title: Experimental vs Theoretical Probability
subtitle: Comparing experiments and the law of large numbers
intro: This lesson connects the two ideas you've met so far: theoretical probability (what the math predicts) and experimental probability (what you actually observe when you run trials). They rarely match exactly, especially in small samples, and that gap is normal random variation — not a broken coin or a wrong theory.

The key principle is the law of large numbers: as the number of trials grows, the experimental probability tends to settle close to the theoretical value. That's why a larger sample gives a more reliable estimate of the true probability.

questions.question: Which of these best describes EXPERIMENTAL probability?
options: A value predicted by reasoning about equally likely outcomes
A value found by actually running trials and recording the results
A probability that is always exactly 0.5
A number that can never be measured

remediation.question: You flip a coin 100 times and use the results to estimate the chance of heads. This estimate is an example of:
Experimental probability is calculated from:

questions.question: Which of these best describes THEORETICAL probability?
options: A value calculated by reasoning: favorable outcomes divided by total outcomes
A value found only by rolling a real die many times
A value that changes every time you run an experiment
The number of trials you performed

remediation.question: Saying the probability of rolling a 3 is 1/6 because there are 6 equally likely faces is an example of:
Theoretical probability is found by:

questions.question: When you run a probability experiment, the experimental probability usually:
options: comes out close to but not exactly the theoretical value
has nothing to do with the theoretical value
is always larger than the theoretical value

remediation.question: Your experiment gives 0.53 for heads while theory says 0.50. The difference is best explained by:
Theoretical P(rolling a 6) is about 0.167, but your data shows 0.20. This gap is mainly caused by:

questions.question: As you increase the number of trials, the experimental probability tends to:
options: drift further from the theoretical value
move closer to the theoretical value
stay frozen at its very first value

remediation.question: Running more and more coin flips makes the proportion of heads:
With a very large number of trials, the experimental result is expected to be:

questions.question: Which experiment gives a MORE reliable estimate of the true probability?

remediation.question: To best estimate the chance of heads, is it better to flip 15 times or 400 times?
A larger number of rolls generally makes your probability estimate:

questions.question: A die rolled 600 times lands on each face close to 100 times. This tendency for results to settle near the theoretical value as trials increase is called:

remediation.question: After 1000 flips the proportion of heads is very near 0.5. This is an example of:
The principle that many trials make experimental results approach the theoretical value is named the:

questions.question: In only 8 flips you get 6 heads (a proportion of 0.75), far from the theoretical 0.50. The best explanation is:
options: small samples can vary a lot from the theoretical value

remediation.question: In just 6 rolls you never roll a 6. The most reasonable explanation is:
options: the sample is small, so big swings are normal

remediation.question: Results that are far from the expected value are MOST likely when the number of trials is:

questions.question: A fair coin has just landed heads 5 times in a row. What is the probability that the next flip is heads?
options: still 0.5, because each flip is independent
greater than 0.5, because heads is on a streak

remediation.question: Does a run of several heads change the chance of heads on the very next flip of a fair coin?
After rolling four 6s in a row with a fair die, the probability the next roll is a 6 is:

questions.question: The theoretical probability is 0.25. After 1000 trials the experimental probability is 0.247. This result is best described as:
options: expected random variation, consistent with the theory
strong evidence the theory is wrong
a sign that too many trials were run

remediation.question: Theory says 0.20; a large experiment gives 0.205. The best description of this gap is:
options: small random variation, consistent with theory

remediation.question: A large experiment lands very close to the theoretical 0.25 but not exactly. This should make you:

questions.question: You roll a die 12 times, never get a 6, and conclude the die must be unfair. What is the best response?
options: 12 rolls is far too few to conclude the die is unfair
A 6 must be impossible on this die
The theoretical probability of a 6 is 0

remediation.question: After 10 flips you get 7 heads and claim the coin is rigged. The most careful conclusion is:
options: 10 flips is too few to decide; flip many more

remediation.question: To fairly test whether a die is biased, you should:
options: roll it a very large number of times
roll it just a few times
